claim: Claude 3.7 Sonnet introduces extended thinking mode where you control token budget (up to 128k thinking tokens) to trade cost/latency against answer quality
actionable: For complex problems, set thinking budget to 30-50k tokens; for simple tasks, use 5-10k; for optimization problems, go higher - monitor cost vs quality tradeoff

claim: Extended thinking uses parallel test-time compute - sampling multiple independent thought processes and then using majority voting or LLM-as-judge to select best answer
actionable: When using thinking mode, request multiple independent reasoning paths; let the model select the best answer via consensus rather than relying on a single thought chain

claim: Benchmarks for thinking models must measure answer quality against test-time compute budget, not just final accuracy - more tokens always yields better answers
actionable: When evaluating thinking models, create compute-vs-quality curves showing performance at different token budgets; don't compare apples-to-apples without accounting for compute

claim: Claude 3.7 Sonnet's thinking is visible in raw form, not summarized - this enables debugging misaligned reasoning where outputs are correct but thinking process is flawed
actionable: Inspect raw thinking output when results look wrong; look for misleading intermediate conclusions or flawed logic paths that led to accidentally correct answers

claim: Claude 3.7 is substantially better at code generation than previous versions - generates multi-page working applications from single prompts with minimal bugs
actionable: Use Claude 3.7 for code generation tasks; expect higher code quality, fewer bugs, and fuller implementations than previous Claude versions

claim: Claude 3.7 self-corrects when the developer says 'it's not working' - it re-thinks the problem and implements fixes without requiring explicit error messages
actionable: When coding with Claude 3.7, describe problems in plain English like 'when I press process nothing happens'; the model will reason through and fix the issue

claim: Claude Code is Anthropic's competitive answer to Cursor - it's a first-generation coding tool that will improve significantly with feedback iteration
actionable: Test Claude Code for comparison with Cursor; expect improvements monthly as Anthropic incorporates user feedback
